questions.questionText: Dendritic corneal ulcer with terminal bulbs, seen with fluorescein staining, is pathognomonic of:
questions.optionB: Herpes simplex keratitis (HSV epithelial keratitis — branching dendritic pattern on rose bengal/fluorescein staining is diagnostic)
questions.explanation: Core concept: Dendritic ulcer with terminal bulbs = HSV keratitis. Why correct: HSV type 1 → epithelial keratitis → characteristic branching dendritic ulcer with terminal club-shaped bulbs. Fluorescein stains the base; rose bengal stains the edges. Why A wrong: Bacterial — stromal infiltrate, mucopurulent discharge, no dendritic pattern. Why C wrong: Fungal — satellite lesions, feathery edges, history of vegetative matter injury. Why D wrong: Acanthamoeba — ring infiltrate, severe pain disproportionate to signs, contact lens wearer. Clinical relevance: Treatment: topical acyclovir 3% ophthalmic ointment 5×/day. DO NOT use steroids in epithelial disease (worsens). Steroids only for STROMAL (immune) keratitis. Exam trap: Dendritic = HSV (terminal bulbs present); pseudodendritic = HZV (no terminal bulbs). This distinction is critical. Memory aid: 'HSV = Herpes Simply shows Dendrites (tree-like branching).' Reference: Khurana – Cornea.
questions.conceptTag: Dendritic ulcer HSV keratitis fluorescein terminal bulbs
